segments.text: Now, 2.3.10, the Buddha said,
monks, these two conditions have part in knowledge, what two?
Samata and Vipassana, tranquilization and contemplation.
If cultivated, what profit does Samata or tranquilization attain?
The mind is cultivated.
What profit results from a cultivated mind?
All lust is abandoned.
Monks, if contemplation Vipassana is cultivated,
what profit does it attain?
Inside is cultivated.
If inside is cultivated, what profit does it attain?
All ignorance is abandoned.
A mind defiled by lust is not set free.
Nor can insight defiled by ignorance be cultivated.
Indeed monks, this seizing of lust is the mind's release.
This seizing of lust is liberation by mind.
This seizing of ignorance is liberation by wisdom.
That's the end of the Sutta.
This is one of the Sutta's which are quite important
as far as meditation is concerned.
Because here, the Buddha is saying that two conditions bring us knowledge.
Samata and Vipassana.
The Buddha didn't say one.
The Buddha said two conditions result in knowledge.
Samata and Vipassana.
And then the Buddha says that by cultivating Samata,
finally the lust is abandoned.
And by cultivating Vipassana, you get insight.
And then finally all ignorance is abandoned.
Sometimes people translate this word Vipassana as insight.
But here you notice that Vipassana if cultivated results in insight or wisdom.
So Vipassana cannot be inside or wisdom.
Vipassana is something that is practiced and will result in insight or wisdom.
Therefore, the proper translation should be contemplation.
In Chinese, it is translated as Julkwan, Julkwan for Samata
and Kwan contemplation for Vipassana.
And then finally the Buddha is saying that
a mind defiled by lust is not set free.
Nor can insight defiled by ignorance be cultivated.
In other words, the Buddha is saying that both are necessary.
That lust must be abandoned and ignorance must be abandoned.
That's why the Buddha is saying that both Samata and Vipassana are necessary.
This word Samata tranquilization is the work of making the mind tranquil.
And there are different levels for different stages of areahood.
In two sutras in the Angutorani Kaya, the Buddha says
that the Sutta Panna and the Sakadagami, first and second stage area,
they only have perfect Sila.
Whereas the Anagami has perfect Sila and Samadhi.
And the Arahana has perfect Sila, Samadhi and Paniya.
So you can see that Anagami and Arahana have perfect Samadhi
which is always stated in the Sutta.
Two beautiful Janas, or at least one point in this of mind.
So from here, you can see that Sutta Panna and Sakadagami
in the Angutorani Kaya does not require Janas.
So from here, you can see that Samata, the practice of tranquilizing the mind,
has different levels for Sutta Panna, Sakadagami and Arahana.
Samata also leads to the lowering of the reduction of the hindrances.
And in the Sangyuta and the Angutorani Kaya,
the Buddha says the condition for seeing things as they really are, is Samadhi.
So nowadays, sometimes we have some controversy over the people
who practice Samata and people who practice Vipassana.
But sometimes people get confused.
The controversy should not be about whether Samata is better or Vipassana is better
because both are necessary in the Buddha's teaching.
If there is any controversy, it should be whether the Buddha's teaching
is to practice both Samata and Vipassana.
Or is it only that one is necessary?
Just as nowadays, some people are saying that only Vipassana is necessary,
but Samata is not necessary.
For this, we see in one Sutta in Angutorani Kaya, 4.170,
where the Arahana, Ananda, Asetana and all is.
There are only four ways to Arahana Buddha.
The first way is Samata first followed by Vipassana.
The second method is Vipassana first followed by Samata.
The third is Samata and Vipassana at the same time.
And the fourth is that you contemplate on the Self,
and then you attain 1.2ness of mind.
And then the way is made clear and then you realize the way to Arahana Buddha.
So from this Sutta, we find that both Samata and Vipassana are necessary.
And there is no way in the Sutta stated that
you can attain Arahana Buddha without 1.2ness of mind or jahanas as was taught by the Buddha.
Now the other day I did not explain what you mean by liberation, by mind and liberation, by insight.
These are the two ways a person attains Arahana Buddha.
And it is if we study the Sutta all the Nikayas,
then we find that there are three ways in which a person attains Arahana Buddha.
One is the person attains the four Rupa jahanas,
and then the four Rupa jahanas,
and then after that a cessation of perception and feeling.
And coming out from a cessation of perception and feeling,
then he attains this liberation.
And the second one is your attains the four jahanas,
and coming out of the four jahanas he contemplates,
a four noble truths etc., and he becomes an Arahana.
The third way a person becomes an Arahana according to the Sutta is when he does not use the jahanas
at the moment of liberation, like the case of the Arahana Sariputta.
A Sariputta according to one Sutta was fanning the Buddha,
and the Buddha was talking to an external sect ascetic.
And because of hearing the Dhamma, as the Buddha explained the Dhamma to the external sect ascetic,
Sariputta just from hearing, he attains Arahana and it is commented by the commentaries
that Sariputta was liberated by wisdom.
Now there is one Sutta in the Majima Nikaya,
where the Buddha said that Sariputta had attained all the jahanas,
all the four Rupa jahanas, all the four Rupa jahanas,
plus cessation of perception and feeling on the road near Oda Samapati.
And so a person who is liberated by wisdom,
even though he does not use jahana at the moment of liberation,
it does not mean that he does not have jahana.
All Arahans have jahana because the Buddha said in two Sutta's in the Guterani Kaya,
that Sutta Panna and Sakadagami, they have perfect Sila.
Anagami have perfect Sila plus Samadhi,
and Arahana has perfect Sila Samadhi and wisdom Sila Samadhi and Paniya.
So in the Sutta's, the perfect Samadhi is always defined either as the four jahanas or one-pointedness of mind.
So the difference here between liberation by mind and liberation by insight or wisdom
is that liberation by mind is the first two cases where they attain the jahanas
and coming out of the jahanas, they contemplate and they become liberated and become Arahans.
So that is liberation by mind.
Whereas liberation by insight is not using the jahanas at the moment of liberation,
but because the mind is very clear due to the cultivation of jahana previously,
then just from contemplation itself, that person, the T.A. and Salahan would.
So this is the liberation by mind and liberation by wisdom.
If you are interested in further details, you can look it up in my booklet,
or some other time we pass it now, where it's explained more in detail there.
Now the other thing about this Sutta I would like to remind is it has to be read in conjunction with another Sutta,
a Guthrani Kaya 6.29.
In that Sutta 6.29, I stated that the best condition for insight is a mind that is so concentrated
that it is bright, a mind that is fully concentrated and is very bright.
Now that is the best condition for insight.
In other words, I am saying that samata, the practice of samata,
tranquilization of the mind, will give you insight or wisdom.
And the other thing mentioned in that Sutta is that to abandon lust,
it is very good to practice contemplation on the 32 parts of the body.
Now this contemplation of the 32 parts of the body is Vipassana practice contemplation.
So in other words, here it is saying, and in this Sutta 6.29,
that practicing of 32 parts of the body contemplation or Vipassana
leads to the abandonment of lust.
And the practice of samata, until the mind becomes concentrated and bright,
is the best condition for insight.
Now this is quite the opposite of the Sutta and just read just now,
because just now that Sutta says that when you practice samata, tranquilization,
it leads to the abandonment of lust.
And Vipassana will bring about insight.
So that is why Sutta has to be compared if you just read one,
you get a one-sided picture.
So basically, what these two Sutta also say is that samata and Vipassana
are both necessary for knowledge or insight.
Now we come to the next Sutta 2.4.1, the Buddha said monks.
I will teach you the condition of the unworthy and that of the worthy.
Do you listen to it?
Attend closely and I will speak.
Yes, Lord, reply these monks to the exalted one.
The exalted one said monks.
The unworthy man is ungrateful, forgetful of benefits.
This ingratitude, this forgetfulness is congenial to mean people.
It is altogether a feature of unworthy people,
this ingratitude and forgetfulness of benefits.
But monks, the worthy person is grateful and mindful of benefits done to him.
This gratitude, this mindfulness, is congenial to the best people.
It is altogether a feature of the best people, this gratitude and mindfulness of benefits.
It is the end of the Sutta.
This Sutta is telling us that we should be grateful to those people
who have benefited us, especially our parents, our elders, our teachers,
monks who have taught us, etc.
And we should know that a person who is ungrateful, according to the Buddha,
is a mean person, especially people like our parents whom we owe a lot.
And you can see this in the next Sutta.
The next Sutta, 2.4.2, the Buddha said monks.
One can never repay two persons, I declare, what two, mother and father?
Even if one should carry about his mother on one shoulder
and his father on the other, and so doing should live a hundred years, a 10, a hundred years
and if he should support them, anointing them with anguines,
that is fragrant oil or something, eating, bathing and rubbing their limbs.
And they, meanwhile, should even void their excrements upon him.
Even so, could he not repay his parents?
Moreover, monks, if he should establish his parents in supreme authority
in the absolute rule over this mighty earth, abounding in the seven treasures,
not even thus, could he repay his parents?
What is the cause of that?
Monks, parents do much for their children, they bring them up, they nourish them,
they introduce them to this world.
Moreover, monks, who so incites his unbelieving parents,
settles and establishes them in the faith,
who so incites his immoral parents, settles and establishes them in morality,
who so incites his stingy parents, settles and establishes them in liberality,
who so incites his foolish parents, settles and establishes them in wisdom,
such a one, just by so doing, does repay, does more than repay what is due to his parents,
that's the end of the sutta.
So here the Buddha is saying that if we should take care of our parents for a hundred years,
taking good care of them, bathing and massaging them, etc,
carrying them about on your shoulder, even for a hundred years,
still cannot pay back the debt of the parents, to your parents,
because when we are young, if all of us try to recall,
we know on many occasions when we were young and helpless,
and we were sick many times, we could have passed away,
not for the great care that our parents showed on earth,
especially our mother, all of us,
we know that the mother usually takes more care of the children personally,
and then the father, that's why in the Indian tradition,
they always talk about Mata, P2, mother-first, mother-father,
whereas in the Chinese tradition, we always say father-first and then mother.
And so you see, even if we were to take good care of our parents until they died,
it's difficult to repay their kindness.
So you can think of a person who is not filial to his parents,
somebody who does not take care of the parents,
and after the parent has passed away,
I have seen for myself somebody who was not very filial to the parent,
and after the parent passed away, the remorse,
it's very great on that person,
because the feeling that you have not done what you should have done
to somebody whom you owe so much, it weighs down heavily on your conscience.
And the last part, the Buddha said,
if we want to repay our parents, there are four ways where we can repay them,
and repay them fully, and even more than what is new.
One is, if they do not have faith in the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha,
you bring them to listen to the teachings of the Buddha,
boy, you lend them tapes to listen, etc.,
and so that they have faith in the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.
Then you have one way, you have repaid your parents.
The second one, if they do not know how to keep the precepts,
they don't know about Sila, you teach them to keep the five precepts.
That's another way you prepare them so that they establish in Sila.
The third way is, if they are stingy,
they don't know how to do dhana offerings,
then you teach them to like, to do charity, to be generous.
That's the third way.
The fourth one, if they do not have wisdom,
and you teach them the Dharma,
or you get them to listen to cassettes, etc.,
of the Buddha's teachings, so that they understand.
And then the wisdom grows.
These are the four ways that anyone of them,
or all of them we can use to pay our parents kindness.
Why are these four ways important?
Because you bring the parents into the right path,
into the right path by having any one of these four,
if they are well established in any one of these four things,
faith in the Buddha, Dharma, Sangha,
keeping the Sila precepts.
Third one, being generous,
liking to do dhana offerings,
and the fourth one is having wisdom,
then at least they will not fall into the woeful claims in the next life.
And if they are well established, especially like wisdom,
they might never ever fall into the woeful claims again,
and slowly get out of Sangsara.
Now the next Sutta is 2.4.4.
Now the house father Anatha Pindika came to visit the exalted one
and saluted him and sat down at one side.
So seated he said this to the exalted one,
pray Lord, how many in the world are worthy of offerings,
and where should an offering be made?
Then the Buddha answered,
2 in the world house father are worthy of offerings,
the learner and the adapt.
These 2 are worthy of offerings in the world,
and here an offering should be made,
and that's the end of the Sutta.
Now this is one of the Sutta's that we need to comment on,
otherwise people may have some wrong bias interpretation
or extreme interpretations.
The Buddha is saying that 2 types of persons are worthy of offerings,
and these 2 types are Aryan persons.
The first one is a learner,
a learner is what is called a Saka,
S-E-K-H-A.
Saka means one who is training,
and one who is training is a person who has attained the first path,
or the first fruit,
or the second path, or the second fruit,
the third path, or the third fruit,
and the fourth path.
These are the 7 types of persons,
and the adapt is the R-Saka,
A-S-E-K-H-A.
Saka is one who is no more learning,
that means an Arahann.
Arahann is a fourth fruit person.
These are the 8 Aryans,
it is not the 4 Aryans,
they are 8 Aryans.
Later on we can see some Sutta's,
which can prove that you have 8 persons actually,
not for some teachings and some related books,
I think the Abhidharma and commentaries.
We say that when a person attains the path,
he immediately attains the fruit,
but later I can show you some Sutta's,
where it is not quite solar.
You can have a person who is on the path,
and he can be on the path for many years,
before he attains the fruit.
So here the Buddha is saying that these Aryans are worthy of offering,
and it is partly because of a Sutta like this,
that there are some people,
due to the greed for merit,
sometimes they only like to make offerings,
two monks, that they think are Aryans,
and they think that other monks are not worthy of offerings,
and this is not the Buddha's teachings.
The Buddha has said in some other Sutta,
I think in the Angutorani Kaya,
that if you make offerings to monks
with a selfish motive to get such and such merit,
then it reduces your merit.
That's one thing we have to know.
The other thing is,
the Buddha has stated in the Angutorani Kaya 4.60
that it is the lay person's duty
to supply the Sangha with the four requisites,
the four requisites that monks need,
are the four essentials of existence.
The first one is food, the second one is robes,
the third one is a place to stay,
and the fourth one is medicine or medical care,
and the Buddha said it is lay person's duty
to supply monks with these four requisites,
and if lay person's don't take care of the Sangha,
then who is the Sangha to turn to,
and they might as well disrobe and earn their own salary
if they people don't support them.
And another thing is,
there's another Sutta where the Buddha said in the future,
I don't have the number of the Sutta of hand,
the Buddha said in the future, there will be monks
who wear the yellow robe,
and they might be corrupt,
they might not keep the precepts,
and they might not practice the spiritual path, etc.
Even then, the Buddha said,
if you make offerings to them,
still it is meritorious.
Why?
Because if we make offerings to the Sangha,
then it helps to perpetuate the Sangha.
And even if at a certain state,
the Sangha do not practice well later,
there's always a chance that you might get our hands again.
Just like in Sri Lanka,
in certain countries, there was a time
when the monks were corrupt.
And so, some people were disillusioned
that some monks went to Burma,
went to Thailand to re-ordain,
and come back and establish a new chapter,
a new sect of Sangha.
Like for example, the Siam Nikaya in Sri Lanka.
It was started after a monk went over to Thailand,
re-ordain with the Thai Sangha and came back.
He wanted to change the Sangha.
So, it is important for lay people to continue to support the Sangha,
so that the Sangha can continue.
If it is cut off, then it is forever cut off.
And the Buddha said that it's always more meritorious
to offer to the Sangha than a single monk.
That's why we noticed in the Vinaya,
the Buddha's mother,
that means the lady who looked after him as a mother,
actually his foster mother,
because his real mother died very soon after he was born.
And the foster mother became a nun known as Mahapajapati.
And after she became a nun,
she decided to make a rope and offer it to the Buddha.
So she came and because of the love for the Buddha and the respect,
she personally offered this rope to the Buddha.
And the Buddha out of compassion for her refused to accept.
The Buddha said, please offer it to the Sangha,
because the Buddha wanted her to have more merit.
But because of some attachment to the Buddha, the love,
she didn't want to offer it to the Sangha.
Even though the merit might be less,
she wanted to give it to the Buddha.
Second time she came and offered it to the Buddha.
Second time the Buddha refused her to give it to the Sangha.
Third time she came and offered to the Buddha.
Third time the Buddha refused her to give it to the Sangha.
Both also had mutual love for each other.
One wanted to give it to him personally.
The other one out of love for her also wanted her to have more merit
and offer it to the Sangha.
The other thing is,
it is stated in the Sutra that of all the material offerings
that a person can give the highest material offering
is to make a monastery either to offer the land for a monastery
or to help build a monastery for the Sangha.
And the Buddha said, by offering a monastery to the Sangha,
the merit is higher than feeding the Buddha
and all the Arhan disciples.
All the Arhan disciples together feeding them
is not so meritorious as offering a monastery for the Sangha to live.
Why? Because by offering a place for the Sangha to live,
then the Sangha is perpetuated.
It continues years and years and years.
Now the other thing is,
lay people should not discriminate too much between monks.
The Buddha said in one Sutra that lay people should not be too attached
to one particular monk.
The details are given in that Sutra.
There is no need to mention it here.
And why? Because it is very difficult for lay people
to distinguish between monks.
And in the Sutras we find even an Anagami,
like the lay person Chitta.
Even though he is an Anagami,
when he invites monks to his house,
he bows down to them.
Even though they are putujana,
even he is an Anagami, he bows to the monks
because he is bowing not to the particular monk,
he is bowing to the Sangha.
The monks represent the Sangha.
In the Angutanikaya 4.4,
the Buddha says that if you treat four persons badly,
you have wrong conduct towards four persons.
You create much remarried.
One is to the Buddha.
The second one is to his disciples.
That means he is among disciples.
The third one is mother, the fourth one is father.
